threats.title: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves Returns (0,0) Due to Missing Return Statement
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves() declares local variables via tuple destructuring of pair.getReserves(), but uses unnamed return values and has no explicit return statement. The function always returns (0, 0). This causes latestAnswer() to compute `minAnswer * (0 + 0) / totalSupply = 0`, making any Cauldron using this oracle value all LP collateral at zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function signature `returns (uint256, uint256)` uses unnamed returns. The tuple `(uint256 baseReserve, uint256 quoteReserve) = pair.getReserves()` creates local variables that shadow unnamed return values. Without an explicit `return` statement, unnamed return values remain at their default (0). Documentation confirms this oracle is designed for Cauldron LP collateral pricing.
deployment_assumptions: MagicLpAggregator is deployed and wired as oracle for a Cauldron with LP collateral
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states: 'MagicLpAggregator: An oracle used to price MagicLP collateral for Cauldrons'. No deployment script evidence of actual deployment found.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function declares local variables via tuple destructuring but has no return statement; unnamed return values default to (0, 0)

preconditions: MagicLpAggregator deployed and used as oracle source for a Cauldron
Pair has non-zero reserves and totalSupply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: function _getReserves() internal view virtual returns (uint256, uint256) { (uint256 baseReserve, uint256 quoteReserve) = pair.getReserves(); }
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Local variables baseReserve/quoteReserve are not returned; unnamed return values stay at 0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit return statement

analysis_questions: Is this oracle currently deployed and used by any active Cauldron?
Are there any subclasses that override _getReserves correctly?
What happens to existing borrowers if exchangeRate becomes 0?
validation_requirements: Call latestAnswer() on a deployed MagicLpAggregator and verify it returns 0
Trace the oracle integration path to any active Cauldron
Test impact on _isSolvent when exchangeRate is 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If exchangeRate is set to 0 via this oracle, the solvency check RHS becomes 0, making all users always solvent. An attacker deposits minimal LP collateral and borrows maximum MIM, since _isSolvent always passes when exchangeRate=0.
prerequisites: MagicLpAggregator integrated as oracle for an active Cauldron

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of MIM from any Cauldron using this oracle
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of affected Cauldrons; protocol-wide bad debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires oracle replacement and potential protocol recapitalization

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies _getReserves returns actual values

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable by calling latestAnswer() off-chain

threats.title: FeeCollectable.setFeeParameters Validates Old State Instead of New Input
threats.hypothesis_description: setFeeParameters() checks `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of `if (_feeBips > BIPS)`. It validates the current state variable rather than the new input parameter. This allows setting feeBips to any uint16 value (up to 65535 = 655.35%) as long as the current value is valid. The first call always passes since initial feeBips is 0. Once set above BIPS, calculateFees() will cause underflow (userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount where feeAmount > amountIn) and subsequent setFeeParameters calls will permanently revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable name mismatch between `feeBips` (state) and `_feeBips` (parameter) is a classic copy-paste or naming error. The validation is demonstrably wrong - it protects the wrong variable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation checks `feeBips > BIPS` (state variable) instead of `_feeBips > BIPS` (input parameter)

preconditions: Caller has onlyAllowedFeeOperator role
Any contract inherits FeeCollectable

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (feeBips > BIPS) { revert ErrInvalidFeeBips(); } followed by feeBips = _feeBips;
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: feeBips (old state) is validated instead of _feeBips (new input parameter)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation of _feeBips value

analysis_questions: Which contracts inherit FeeCollectable and are deployed?
What is the current feeBips value in deployed instances?
What is the impact on calculateFees if feeBips > BIPS?
validation_requirements: Call setFeeParameters with _feeBips > 10000 when current feeBips <= 10000
Verify the call succeeds and feeBips is set to the invalid value
Test calculateFees with feeBips > 10000 to confirm underflow/DoS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Fee operator calls setFeeParameters(collector, 20000). Since current feeBips is 0, the check (0 > 10000) is false, allowing the set. Now feeBips=20000 (200%). calculateFees computes feeAmount = amountIn * 20000 / 10000 = 2*amountIn, causing underflow in userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount. Any subsequent setFeeParameters call also reverts (20000 > 10000), permanently locking the broken fee.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS of fee-related functionality; potential fund extraction if fee > 100% is processable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of contracts inheriting FeeCollectable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Irrecoverable once set - no function can fix feeBips since validation blocks all future calls

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test validates input parameter vs state variable in setFeeParameters

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by reading feeBips state variable

threats.title: Router._validateDecimals Underflows When baseDecimals > quoteDecimals
threats.hypothesis_description: Router._validateDecimals performs `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` as uint8 subtraction. In Solidity 0.8+, this underflows and reverts when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals. This prevents creating any pool via Router where the base token has more decimals than the quote token, even if the absolute difference is within acceptable range (e.g., base=18, quote=8).
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation is asymmetric - it only works when quoteDecimals >= baseDecimals. The developer likely intended to check absolute difference but performed one-directional subtraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: quoteDecimals - baseDecimals underflows as uint8 when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

preconditions: User attempts to create a pool where base token decimals > quote token decimals
Using Router.createPool or Router.createPoolETH

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE) - uint8 subtraction underflows
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Asymmetric validation blocks legitimate pool creation for certain decimal combinations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No handling for baseDecimals > quoteDecimals case

analysis_questions: What token pairs are intended to be created via Router?
Can pools be created directly via Factory, bypassing Router validation?
Are there deployed pools with baseDecimals > quoteDecimals?
validation_requirements: Attempt createPool with base=WBTC(8 decimals) and quote=USDC(6 decimals) - should work but reverts
Verify that Factory.create works directly without this limitation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attack but a code defect. Any user trying to create a pool where base has more decimals than quote will have the transaction revert. They would need to swap the token order or use Factory.create directly.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS for pool creation with certain token pairs via Router
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Liquidity providers using the Router
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users can use Factory.create directly as a workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for createPool with base decimals > quote decimals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately observable when attempting pool creation

threats.title: DegenBox.transferMultiple Only Validates First Recipient Address
threats.hypothesis_description: DegenBox.transferMultiple() checks `require(tos[0] != address(0))` but does not validate subsequent recipients. Shares transferred to address(0) at indices > 0 are permanently locked, as no mechanism exists to recover them from the zero address balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation explicitly checks only tos[0]. In a batch transfer, any approved masterContract clone could construct a transferMultiple call that burns user shares to address(0) at index > 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only tos[0] is checked for zero address; tos[1..n] can be address(0)

preconditions: Caller has allowed(from) permission
tos array has length > 1 with address(0) at index > 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: require(tos[0] != address(0)) but no check for subsequent elements
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Shares sent to address(0) at indices > 0 are permanently burned/locked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing validation loop for all recipients

analysis_questions: Can CauldronV4 cook's ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER_MULTIPLE be used to trigger this?
What masterContract clones could call this function?
Is there any mechanism to recover shares from address(0)?
validation_requirements: Call transferMultiple with tos = [validAddr, address(0)] and verify shares are lost
Confirm no recovery mechanism for shares at address(0)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An approved masterContract clone constructs a transferMultiple call with tos=[legitimate_addr, address(0)]. Shares at index 1 are credited to address(0) and permanently locked. This could be triggered via CauldronV4's cook ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER_MULTIPLE.
prerequisites: Approved masterContract clone or direct caller with allowed(from)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of transferred shares at zero address indices
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose approved masterContract clones construct such transfers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - shares at address(0) cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test validates all recipients in transferMultiple

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via balance tracking at address(0)

threats.title: Liquidation Double allBorrowShare Computation Exploitable via Malicious Swapper
threats.hypothesis_description: In CauldronV4.liquidate(), allBorrowShare is computed from BentoBox.toShare() before transferring collateral and calling the swapper, then recomputed after the swap. The swapper callback can modify BentoBox's MIM totals (elastic/base ratio), causing the second toShare computation to return a different value. If the swapper manipulates BentoBox totals to decrease the share requirement, the protocol receives fewer MIM shares than the actual debt requires, creating bad debt.
threats.investigation_rationale: The first allBorrowShare computation is passed to the swapper as shareToMin. After the swap, allBorrowShare is recomputed with potentially changed BentoBox state. This pattern violates the principle of computing once and using consistently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: allBorrowShare computed at line 351, swapper called at line 354, allBorrowShare recomputed at line 356

preconditions: Liquidator provides a custom swapper contract
Swapper can interact with BentoBox during callback (deposit/withdraw MIM to change totals)
Insolvent positions exist

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bentoBox.toShare(MIM, allBorrowAmount, true) computed twice with swapper.swap() call in between
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Swapper can change BentoBox MIM elastic/base ratio during callback
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No snapshot of share ratio before swap

analysis_questions: Can the swapper deposit/withdraw MIM in BentoBox during the callback?
What magnitude of ratio change is achievable within the swap callback?
Is BentoBox reentrancy possible from the swapper context?
validation_requirements: Implement a malicious swapper that deposits MIM into BentoBox during callback
Measure the difference between first and second allBorrowShare computations
Calculate net profit/loss for the attacker

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Liquidator deploys a malicious swapper. During swap callback, the swapper deposits a large amount of MIM into BentoBox, increasing the elastic amount relative to base. This makes toShare() return fewer shares for the same amount. The second allBorrowShare is smaller than the first. Liquidator pays fewer shares than required, profiting from the difference while leaving the protocol undercollateralized.
prerequisites: Flash loan for MIM deposit into BentoBox during swap

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bad debt creation; protocol receives less MIM than owed per liquidation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol and all MIM depositors in the BentoBox
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Bad debt accumulates over time

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies allBorrowShare consistency across swap callback

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~500K gas for liquidation + swap + BentoBox manipulation
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Flash loan for MIM manipulation in BentoBox
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing expected vs actual MIM shares received

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards processExpiredLocks lastLockIndex Corruption via Swap-and-Pop
threats.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks uses swap-and-pop to remove expired locks. When lastLockIndex points to the last array element and a non-last element is processed, the last element is swapped to the processed position and lastLockIndex is updated. However, if a user has multiple locks and the operator processes them in certain orders, lastLockIndex may point to an older lock instead of the most recently created one. This causes _createLock to create new lock entries instead of appending to the latest, potentially hitting maxLocks prematurely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The swap-and-pop operation at line moves locks[lastIndex] to locks[index], and updates lastLockIndex if it was lastIndex. But the swapped lock may have an older unlockTime, and subsequent _createLock checks compare the lock at lastLockIndex against nextUnlockTime.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap-and-pop modifies lastLockIndex but may point to chronologically older lock after processing

preconditions: User has multiple active locks
Operator processes a non-last expired lock
User subsequently creates new locks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (lastLockIndex[user] == lastIndex) { lastLockIndex[user] = index; } - points to swapped (older) lock
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After swap, lastLockIndex may reference a lock with older unlockTime, causing _createLock to always push new entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No verification that lastLockIndex points to chronologically newest lock after processing

analysis_questions: How does _createLock behave when lastLockIndex points to an older lock?
Can this cause a user to hit maxLocks limit prematurely?
Does this affect reward accounting?
validation_requirements: Create scenario: user has 3 locks, process the first, verify lastLockIndex after swap-and-pop
Attempt new lock creation and verify it creates new entry vs appending
Check if maxLocks limit is hit earlier than expected

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User creates locks at weeks 1, 2, 3 (indices 0, 1, 2, lastLockIndex=2). At week 14, lock 0 expires. Operator processes index 0. Lock 2 is swapped to index 0, lastLockIndex updated to 0. Lock at index 0 now has unlockTime from week 3's lock. User tries to lock again at week 15: _createLock sees lock at lastLockIndex(0) has unlockTime < nextUnlockTime, creates new entry instead of appending. Over time, user hits maxLocks limit and cannot lock more tokens.
prerequisites: User with multiple locks across different epochs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users unable to lock tokens due to hitting maxLocks prematurely
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Active stakers with multiple lock cycles
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operator must process all expired locks to free slots

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lock index consistency after out-of-order processing

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable by checking userLocks array length vs expected

threats.title: CauldronV4 Blacklist Becomes Stale After BentoBox Ownership Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: CauldronV4 constructor and init() blacklist the current BentoBox owner: `blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] = true`. This captures the owner address at deployment time. If BentoBox ownership is later transferred, the new owner is NOT blacklisted. An attacker could use ACTION_CALL in cook() to call the new BentoBox owner address, which may have special privileges (e.g., if the owner is a multisig or governance contract with executor capabilities).
threats.investigation_rationale: The blacklist is static - set once at init and never updated for ownership changes. The BentoBox owner is blacklisted because they have special permissions. A new owner would inherit these permissions but not be blacklisted.
deployment_assumptions: BentoBox ownership must be transferred after Cauldron deployment
threats.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol shows BentoBox ownership transfer: `BoringOwnable(address(blastBox)).transferOwnership(owner, true, false)` after Cauldron deployment

proof_of_signal.pattern: blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] = true set only at init; not updated on ownership changes

preconditions: BentoBox ownership transferred after Cauldron init
New BentoBox owner address has callable functions exploitable via ACTION_CALL

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Blacklist of BentoBox owner captured at deployment time, becomes stale after ownership transfer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: New BentoBox owner not blacklisted; may have functions that interact with BentoBox privileges
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No dynamic blacklist update mechanism for BentoBox owner

analysis_questions: Has BentoBox ownership been transferred after Cauldron deployment?
What functions does the new BentoBox owner expose that could be called via ACTION_CALL?
Could calling the new owner's functions affect BentoBox state in a way that benefits the attacker?
validation_requirements: Check if deployed BentoBox owner differs from the address blacklisted at init
Enumerate callable functions on the new owner address
Test ACTION_CALL to new owner address

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After BentoBox ownership transfers from address A to address B, Cauldron still blacklists A but not B. Attacker uses cook() with ACTION_CALL targeting address B. If B is a governance contract, the attacker could trigger governance actions through the Cauldron's context.
prerequisites: New owner has exploitable callable functions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential unauthorized operations through BentoBox owner's privileges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All Cauldron and BentoBox users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: MasterContract owner can manually blacklist the new address via setBlacklistedCallee

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for blacklist staleness after ownership transfer

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing blacklisted addresses vs current BentoBox owner

threats.title: MagicLP Uses tx.origin for Fee Rate Determination Instead of msg.sender
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP.sellBase(), sellQuote(), and flashLoan() pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate calculation. This means fees are determined by the EOA that initiated the transaction, not the actual caller. Smart contract wallets, aggregators, and meta-transaction relayers will get fees based on the EOA rather than the trading entity, breaking fee model isolation.
threats.investigation_rationale: tx.origin is used at sellBase line reading querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) and similarly in sellQuote and flashLoan. If the fee model offers different rates based on trader address (e.g., whitelisted addresses), the fee bypasses contract-level identity.
deployment_assumptions: Fee rate model provides differentiated rates based on trader address
threats.config_evidence: IFeeRateModel.getFeeRate(trader, lpFeeRate) takes trader as parameter, suggesting trader-dependent rates are possible

proof_of_signal.pattern: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee lookup

preconditions: Fee rate model provides different rates based on trader address
Trades routed through contracts (aggregators, smart wallets)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: tx.origin passed as trader parameter to fee model
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Smart contract callers inherit EOA's fee rate, not their own
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use msg.sender for accurate fee identity

analysis_questions: Does the deployed FeeRateModel provide different rates based on trader address?
What aggregators or smart wallets interact with MagicLP?
Could a whitelisted EOA's fee rate be exploited via contract routing?
validation_requirements: Deploy a fee model with trader-dependent rates
Compare fees when calling directly vs through a contract
Measure fee differential

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the fee model gives lower rates to certain addresses, a trader routes swaps through a contract initiated by a privileged EOA to get lower fees. Alternatively, a relayer submitting meta-transactions would charge fees based on the relayer EOA, not the actual user.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Fee evasion or incorrect fee application; LP revenue reduction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Liquidity providers receiving less fees than expected
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code update to use msg.sender

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing fee rates between direct and contract-mediated swaps

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Fee comparison analysis

threats.title: CauldronV4 cook() can be used to move funds from arbitrary users via BentoBox transfer actions after self-approving a master contract
threats.hypothesis_description: CauldronV4.cook() exposes ACTION_BENTO_SETAPPROVAL (24) allowing the caller to submit an EIP-712 signature to BentoBox approving a master contract to spend the caller’s BentoBox funds. If any weakness exists in BentoBox approval validation (domain separator mismatch, chainId issues, signature malleability, or approving unintended masterContract due to address(0) edge), an attacker could combine approval + ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER/MULTIPLE to exfiltrate shares from victims in the same cook transaction. The Cauldron side does not restrict which master contract can be approved nor ties approvals to msg.sender beyond forwarding parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: cook() composes BentoBox approvals and transfers in a single untrusted entrypoint. While BentoBox’s EIP-712 path is intended to be safe, any subtle signature verification bug or domain mismatch becomes immediately weaponizable as a one-tx drain path using Cauldron as a call router. This is high value to validate because the exploit would be simple and catastrophic if a flaw exists in the approval verification or its integration assumptions.
deployment_assumptions: At least one user can be induced to provide a signature that is valid under BentoBox.setMasterContractApproval for an unintended masterContract/user tuple OR BentoBox signature verification has an implementation flaw
CauldronV4 clone is whitelisted/registered in BentoBox masterContractOf mapping such that cook can call setMasterContractApproval (it does call BentoBox directly; BentoBox validates signature itself)
threats.config_evidence: No explicit deployment evidence in provided scripts that CauldronV4 is registered/whitelisted in BentoBox; validate BentoBox deployment config and whether Cauldron uses BentoBox approvals in production.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cook() forwards ACTION_BENTO_SETAPPROVAL parameters directly into bentoBox.setMasterContractApproval(user,_masterContract,approved,v,r,s) and then allows arbitrary BentoBox transfers/withdrawals in same call

preconditions: Attacker can call CauldronV4.cook() on a deployed clone
A victim signature or a BentoBox approval-validation weakness exists
BentoBox.transfer/withdraw can be executed for victim funds once approval is in place

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: CauldronV4.sol cook() ACTION_BENTO_SETAPPROVAL branch and subsequent ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER/_WITHDRAW branches
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: single-transaction composition of approval + transfer primitives
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: any approval verification weakness upstream becomes immediate drain path
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Cauldron does not restrict approvable masterContract nor cross-check user==msg.sender before forwarding approval parameters

analysis_questions: Does BentoBox.setMasterContractApproval safely bind (user, masterContract, nonce, chainId, verifyingContract) and reject malleable signatures?
Can ACTION_BENTO_SETAPPROVAL be used with user != msg.sender in a way that still succeeds (e.g., due to signature bugs)?
Is there any path where approving masterContract==address(0) or a clone mapping edge could grant broad approvals?
validation_requirements: Unit test cook() sequences: [SETAPPROVAL -> TRANSFER] attempting to move shares from a third party
Fuzz EIP-712 signature fields and chainId/domain separator transitions
Check BentoBox MasterContractManager implementation actually deployed/used by this Cauldron’s bentoBox

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits cook() that first sets an approval in BentoBox using a crafted/abused signature, then immediately calls ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER to move victim’s shares to attacker-controlled address.
prerequisites: Valid signature exploit vector or BentoBox approval bug

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full theft of victim BentoBox deposits/shares for impacted tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any BentoBox user whose approval can be forged/abused
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low; theft is final on-chain

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of negative tests proving cook() cannot be used to move third-party BentoBox funds without legitimate approvals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain approval + transfer events in same tx

threats.title: CauldronV4 withdrawFees() callable by anyone may be griefable to force feeTo transfer failures or burn opportunities
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawFees() is permissionless and transfers accrued MIM fees to masterContract.feeTo(). If feeTo is misconfigured (e.g., zero address or a contract that rejects BentoBox transfers) or if BentoBox transfer semantics can be influenced to revert, any user can repeatedly attempt withdrawFees and cause persistent reverts, potentially blocking integrations that bundle withdrawFees with other actions (e.g., keeper flows).
threats.investigation_rationale: Permissionless admin-like maintenance functions are commonly grief targets. While this doesn’t directly steal funds, it can lock protocol fee withdrawals or break automation. Validate whether feeTo is always non-zero and whether BentoBox transfers can revert due to recipient hooks or token behavior.
deployment_assumptions: feeTo is unset/zero OR points to an address that cannot receive BentoBox transfers for MIM OR token/BentoBox behavior can revert on transfer
A keeper/automation system relies on withdrawFees success for other actions (operational coupling)
threats.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol sets cauldron.setFeeTo(feeTo) when deploying BlastCauldronV4, but other deployments/clones may omit it; verify masterContract.feeTo() across environments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawFees() is public and uses masterContract.feeTo() without validating non-zero/receivable; transfers via bentoBox.transfer then sets feesEarned=0

preconditions: accrueInfo.feesEarned > 0
feeTo misconfigured or transfer can revert
External systems depend on withdrawFees liveness

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless maintenance function with external transfer to configurable recipient
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert can block fee extraction; recipient validation absent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(_feeTo!=address(0)) and no try/catch around transfer

analysis_questions: Can BentoBox.transfer to feeTo revert due to token/recipient conditions?
Is feeTo guaranteed set for all master contracts and clones?
Do any keeper flows bundle withdrawFees with other state changes that would be blocked by revert?
validation_requirements: Simulate feeTo==address(0) and verify revert behavior
Simulate feeTo as reverting contract (if BentoBox has hooks) or unsupported recipient conditions
Review deployment and clone initialization patterns for feeTo configuration guarantees

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls withdrawFees repeatedly; if misconfigured, every call reverts and may prevent automated maintenance scripts from progressing.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol fees stuck; indirect loss via operational disruption
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol/treasury; potentially users if operations depend on fee withdrawal
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (requires config change by admin)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for feeTo edge cases and permissionless withdrawFees grief

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Repeated reverted calls to withdrawFees

threats.title: DegenBox withdraw(ETH) sends raw ETH via call() enabling reentrancy into other BentoBox operations
threats.hypothesis_description: DegenBox.withdraw() for ETH unwraps WETH and performs a low-level ETH transfer to `to` using `to.call{value: amount}("")`. This external call happens after internal accounting updates, but it enables the recipient to re-enter BentoBox (e.g., call deposit/withdraw/transfer) during the ETH receive hook. If any other function assumes non-reentrancy (especially strategy/harvest or skim-based deposit paths), reentrancy could be used to manipulate `_tokenBalanceOf`, totals accounting, or interact with flashLoan repayment checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even with state-updates-before-interactions, cross-function reentrancy across vault operations is a common source of share-ratio and solvency bugs—especially with skim paths and strategy balances. Validate whether BentoBox/DegenBox relies on implicit non-reentrancy anywhere else or if strategies can be abused during reentry.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ETH withdraw path: IWETH.withdraw(amount); then (bool success,) = to.call{value: amount}("") enabling arbitrary reentrancy

preconditions: Token_ == USE_ETHEREUM path used (withdraw ETH)
Recipient `to` is a contract with a payable fallback/receive that calls back into DegenBox
A reentrant sequence exists that causes inconsistent accounting (to be proven)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external call with value during a critical vault operation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cross-function reentrancy into deposit/withdraw/flashLoan/harvest while totals were just mutated
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant guard across vault entrypoints

analysis_questions: Can reentrant calls exploit skim checks or share/elastic conversions to extract value?
Can reentrancy during ETH withdrawal interfere with flashLoan repayment invariant checks?
Are there any strategies whose harvest/withdraw/skim are callable during reentry and affect accounting?

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards withdraw()/getRewards() lack nonReentrant and transfer tokens to msg.sender enabling callback-based multi-claim/lock inconsistencies
threats.hypothesis_description: LockingMultiRewards performs external token transfers in withdraw() (stakingToken.safeTransfer) and in _getRewards() (rewardToken.safeTransfer) without a reentrancy guard. threats.title: MagicLP buyShares() initial mint logic may be manipulable via pre-seeding balances to extract unfair shares (donation attack)
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP.buyShares() bases initial shares on current on-contract balances (baseBalance/quoteBalance) rather than explicit deposit amounts. If an attacker can pre-seed the pool address with tokens before the first buyShares call (e.g., transfer tokens directly to the yet-to-be-initialized clone address after deterministic creation, or to a clone before any liquidity is minted), the initial LP share calculation can be skewed. While 1001 dead shares are minted to address(0), the formula still trusts raw balances and could grant the first minter a disproportionate share of pooled value relative to their actual intended provision.
threats.investigation_rationale: Donation attacks on AMMs that rely on balance deltas are practical. Deterministic clone addresses (Factory.predictDeterministicAddress / create) can allow third parties to send tokens before initialization/mint, skewing first LP issuance. Validate whether Factory.create is atomic enough to prevent pre-seeding between clone deployment and init, and whether anyone can transfer tokens to the clone address before buyShares in Router flows.

threats.title: cook() Solvency Check Bypass via Unhandled Action Codes
threats.hypothesis_description: The cook() function's solvency check can be entirely bypassed by appending an unhandled action code (e.g., ACTION_ACCRUE=8, or any value like 0, 1, 3, 9) after ACTION_BORROW or ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL. The unhandled action falls to the else branch calling _additionalCookAction(), which returns a default-initialized CookStatus{needsSolvencyCheck:false, hasAccrued:false}. The line `status = returnStatus` overwrites the previously set needsSolvencyCheck=true, causing the final solvency check to be skipped entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect in the most critical financial control of the lending protocol. The solvency check is the sole mechanism preventing undercollateralized borrowing. Its bypass enables unlimited borrowing against minimal or no collateral, draining the entire MIM supply from the Cauldron.

proof_of_signal.pattern: else branch calls _additionalCookAction which returns default CookStatus; status = returnStatus overwrites needsSolvencyCheck=true set by ACTION_BORROW

preconditions: Any CauldronV4 or BlastCauldronV4 clone is deployed and funded with MIM
Attacker has some collateral in BentoBox or uses skim mode
No override of _additionalCookAction that preserves CookStatus

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: status = returnStatus where returnStatus is default CookStatus{false,false} from empty _additionalCookAction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: cook([ACTION_BORROW, 0], ...) - second action is unhandled, resets needsSolvencyCheck to false
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker borrows entire MIM balance of Cauldron with minimal collateral

economic_questions: What is the total MIM supply available across all deployed Cauldron clones?

Can the attack be executed atomically in a single transaction?

threats.title: BlastOnboardingBoot._claimable Treats MIM and USDB as Equal Value
threats.hypothesis_description: _claimable() computes user pool shares by summing raw MIM and USDB locked amounts: totalLocked = totals[MIM].locked + totals[USDB].locked. Since MIM and USDB have different values (I=0.998, meaning 1 MIM ≈ 0.998 USDB), depositing the less valuable token gives disproportionate shares. If MIM depegs further, an attacker buying cheap MIM and depositing it gets the same claim weight as expensive USDB deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bootstrap share allocation does not account for the value difference between MIM and USDB. This creates an economic incentive to deposit whichever token is cheaper per unit, diluting the more expensive token's depositors.

threats.title: Router AddLiquidity Pulls Unintended Excess Tokens from User
threats.hypothesis_description: The Router's `addLiquidity` function can pull significantly more tokens from a user's wallet than they specified, draining their funds if they have granted infinite approval.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `_adjustAddLiquidity`, the code calculates `baseBalance = balanceOf(lp) + baseInAmount` and then overwrites `baseInAmount = baseBalance - baseReserve`. If the LP pool has excess tokens (e.g., from direct transfers or airdrops), this excess is added to the user's `baseInAmount`. The Router then uses this inflated `baseAdjustedInAmount` to call `safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, lp, baseAdjustedInAmount)`. This forces the user to pay for the pool's excess balance out of their own wallet.

threats.title: Strategy Yield Sandwich Attack via Public Harvest
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can extract accumulated strategy yield by flash-loaning tokens, depositing them into DegenBox to gain a massive share of the pool, calling the public `harvest` function to realize the yield, and immediately withdrawing their shares for a profit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `harvest` function in `DegenBox` is public and can be called by anyone. When a strategy generates profit, `harvest` increases `totals[token].elastic`, which instantly increases the value of all existing shares. Because DegenBox has no deposit/withdrawal fees or timelocks, an attacker can sandwich the `harvest` call: deposit huge capital -> call harvest -> withdraw. This dilutes the yield for long-term depositors and transfers it to the attacker.

threats.title: Unregistered Rebasing Tokens Leak Yield via DegenBox Skimming
threats.hypothesis_description: If a rebasing token is enabled in BlastBox but not added to the BlastTokenRegistry, it defaults to AUTOMATIC yield mode. The accrued yield can be stolen by any user via the DegenBox `deposit` skimming feature.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `BlastBox.sol`, `setTokenEnabled` only configures a token to `CLAIMABLE` yield mode if it exists in the `BlastTokenRegistry`. If an admin enables a rebasing token (like USDB or WETH) without registering it first, the token remains in `AUTOMATIC` mode. As the token balance of the DegenBox naturally grows from the yield, the actual balance will exceed `totals[token].elastic`. Any MEV searcher or attacker can call `deposit` with `from = address(this)` to skim this excess balance, converting the protocol's yield into shares for themselves.
deployment_assumptions: A rebasing token is enabled in BlastBox but not added to BlastTokenRegistry
threats.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol lines 56-61 show WETH and USDB are correctly registered, but future tokens might not be.

threats.title: Router Incompatible with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The Router's `removeLiquidityETH` function assumes the exact amount of tokens returned by `sellShares` is received, causing it to revert if the token implements a fee-on-transfer mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Router.sol`, `removeLiquidityETH` calls `IMagicLP(lp).sellShares(...)`, which returns `tokenAmountOut`. The Router then attempts to transfer this exact `tokenAmountOut` to the user via `token.safeTransfer(to, tokenAmountOut)`. If the token is a fee-on-transfer token, the Router will receive `tokenAmountOut - fee` from the MagicLP pool. The subsequent `safeTransfer` will revert because the Router's balance is less than `tokenAmountOut`, permanently trapping the user's liquidity in the pool when using this function.

threats.title: MagicLP fee model can be abused to revert or overcharge swaps by returning extreme fee rates
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP queries an external IFeeRateModel for (lpFeeRate, mtFeeRate) and subtracts both from the computed output. If the fee model returns a combined fee >= 100% of output, subtraction underflows and reverts, halting swaps/flashloan settlement. Even if it doesn't underflow, extreme fees can effectively confiscate output or route it to the maintainer.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical on-chain exploit if the fee model is upgradable/mutable, misconfigured, or malicious. It is not just "admin can rug"—a single erroneous fee model response can brick trading across all pools using it.
deployment_assumptions: maintainerFeeRateModel is upgradeable/mutable, or its implementation can be changed, or its maintainer address can be changed to adversarial behavior
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script MIMSwap.s.sol deploys FeeRateModel and then sets its implementation (FeeRateModel.setImplementation). FeeRateModel code not included here; mutability must be verified in Phase D.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(trader, _LP_FEE_RATE_); receiveAmount = receiveAmount - lpFee - mtFee; // no bounds check on returned rates

threats.title: DegenBox batchFlashLoan can be used to induce partial state/asset movements if borrower misbehaves (no internal reentrancy guard)
threats.hypothesis_description: batchFlashLoan transfers each token to its receiver before calling the borrower callback. If the borrower performs complex nested interactions, and some token repayment checks pass while others fail, the whole transaction reverts (atomic), but operationally the large surface area enables repeated revert-based griefing against protocols that rely on same-block flash liquidity. Additionally, without a reentrancy guard, borrowers can re-enter other DegenBox functions to increase complexity and gas consumption.
threats.investigation_rationale: While reverts are atomic, repeated revert-based griefing is practical on L2 and can disrupt keepers/automation and liquidation bots that depend on flash liquidity. Validate whether any integrated operations assume batchFlashLoan success under adversarial conditions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: transfers occur before borrower.onBatchFlashLoan(...); no reentrancy guard; repayment checked after callback per token

preconditions: Other protocol components or bots rely on batchFlashLoan availability and predictable execution
Attacker can pay gas to submit repeated reverting batchFlashLoan attempts

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Large unpermissioned external callback surface; no explicit reentrancy guard

threats.title: Unclearable Bad Debt Causes Infinite Interest Accrual
threats.hypothesis_description: In liquidate(), if a user's position is heavily underwater, liquidating their full debt requires more collateral than they possess, causing an underflow revert. Liquidators must partially liquidate until collateral reaches zero. The remaining bad debt cannot be cleared because any further liquidation attempts will revert when subtracting from zero collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: This permanently corrupts totalBorrow accounting, causing totalBorrow.elastic to grow infinitely due to interest on uncollectible bad debt, diluting the debt shares of all healthy borrowers.

threats.title: BlastOnboarding deposit cap check performed after state mutation, causing full deposit DoS at/near cap and potential griefing
threats.hypothesis_description: BlastOnboarding.deposit() updates totals and user balances before checking caps[token]. If the cap is exceeded, the function reverts and undoes state, but the token transferFrom has already occurred earlier in the function; while EVM revert rolls back the transfer too, this pattern can still create cap-edge usability issues and makes it impossible to deposit an amount that would cross the cap even if user expects partial acceptance. Attackers can repeatedly submit deposits that revert to waste sequencer throughput and complicate onboarding at the cap boundary.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical grief/DoS vector on a high-traffic bootstrapping event: at the cap boundary, many deposits revert. While not direct theft, it can materially disrupt onboarding operations and user experience in production.

preconditions: state == Opened
token is supported
caps[token] is set and can be reached by user deposits

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cap boundary creates unavoidable revert for any amount that crosses cap; enables repeated revert spam

threats.title: Router _validatePath allows empty path due to dead check, enabling unexpected behavior or downstream revert surfaces
threats.hypothesis_description: Router._validatePath checks `if (pathLength <= 0) revert ErrEmptyPath();` but pathLength is uint256 and the condition is always false. Empty paths (length 0) are not rejected here, leading to out-of-bounds reads later (path[0], path.length-1) and consistent reverts. Attackers can exploit this as a cheap revert spam vector against Router entry points.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect (wrong condition) that should be fixed. While reverts roll back state, it can still be abused for griefing and indicates insufficient input validation.

threats.title: MagicLpAggregator Vulnerable to Flash Loan Spot Price Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `MagicLpAggregator` calculates the LP token price using the spot reserves of the MagicLP pool: `minAnswer * (baseReserve + quoteReserve) / pair.totalSupply()`. An attacker can use a flash loan to heavily imbalance the pool (e.g., swapping a massive amount of base tokens for quote tokens). In a constant-product-like AMM, imbalancing the pool significantly increases the sum of the reserves (`baseReserve + quoteReserve`). This artificially inflates the LP token price reported by the oracle, allowing the attacker to borrow excessive MIM from the Cauldron.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using spot reserves for oracle pricing is a known anti-pattern. Because the sum of reserves increases when the pool is imbalanced, the LP token price can be manipulated upward atomically, enabling profitable read-only reentrancy / flash loan attacks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LP price calculated using spot (baseReserve + quoteReserve)

preconditions: MagicLpAggregator is used as the oracle for a Cauldron
Sufficient flash loan liquidity exists for the pool's base or quote tokens

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan swap to increase (baseReserve + quoteReserve)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker borrows MIM against artificially inflated LP collateral, then repays flash loan, leaving protocol with bad debt

threats.title: Unprotected ETH Wrapping Allows Skimming of Stuck ETH in DegenBox
threats.hypothesis_description: In `DegenBox.deposit`, when `token_ == USE_ETHEREUM`, the contract calls `IWETH.deposit{value: amount}()`. It does not verify that `amount <= msg.value`. Furthermore, the skim check (`amount <= _tokenBalanceOf(token).sub(total.elastic)`) is explicitly bypassed for `USE_ETHEREUM`. This allows any user to call `deposit` with `msg.value = 0` and `amount` equal to the DegenBox's raw ETH balance. The contract will wrap its own stuck ETH into WETH and credit the corresponding shares to the attacker's account.
threats.investigation_rationale: Smart contracts often accumulate stuck ETH due to accidental transfers or selfdestructs. The lack of `msg.value` validation combined with the skim bypass allows anyone to freely extract this stuck ETH.

threats.title: MagicLP sellShares Target Rounding Asymmetry Creates Cumulative Pricing Drift
threats.hypothesis_description: In MagicLP.sellShares(), token amounts returned to the user use floor division (round down), while _BASE_TARGET_ and _QUOTE_TARGET_ reductions use divCeil (round up). This asymmetry means targets decrease faster than proportional to the actual tokens removed, accumulating error over many withdrawals. Over time, targets drift significantly below their correct values, distorting PMM pricing curves.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rounding directions are demonstrably inconsistent: amounts use floor (user gets less) while targets use ceil (targets decrease more). Each operation creates up to 1 wei error per target, but over thousands of operations this could materially affect PMM pricing by distorting the equilibrium reference points.

proof_of_signal.pattern: baseAmount uses floor division (baseBalance * shareAmount / totalShares) but _BASE_TARGET_ uses divCeil: uint112(uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) - (uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * shareAmount).divCeil(totalShares))

preconditions: Pool with active trading and LP withdrawal activity
Many small sellShares operations over time
Sufficient pool lifetime for rounding errors to accumulate

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural usage accumulates error; could be accelerated by many tiny withdrawals
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Distorted targets change PMM pricing, potentially creating arbitrage opportunities between pool price and external price

threats.title: Factory.create Uses tx.origin for Deterministic Salt Preventing Contract-Initiated Pool Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory.create() uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to compute the deterministic clone salt. This means: (1) smart contract wallets and multisigs cannot reliably create pools since the deployer address varies, (2) the same EOA cannot create the same pool through different contracts, and (3) a pool's deterministic address depends on the EOA, not the calling contract. This breaks composability and prevents protocol-to-protocol integrations from deploying pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin for deterministic address computation is a code defect that breaks the fundamental composability pattern. Any contract trying to programmatically create a pool gets a different address than expected if called by different EOAs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address creator = tx.origin; bytes32 salt = _computeSalt(creator, ...) — tx.origin instead of msg.sender

preconditions: Any contract or multisig attempting to create pools through Factory
Pools created through different entry points by same EOA

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: tx.origin dependency makes deterministic addresses unreliable for smart contract callers
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS on automated pool creation; could block BlastOnboardingBoot.bootstrap if called via multisig

threats.title: MagicLP buyShares Permanently DoS'd When One Reserve Depleted to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In MagicLP.buyShares(), when totalSupply() > 0 but one reserve is zero (e.g., quoteReserve == 0), the code falls through both conditional branches: the initial supply branch (totalSupply == 0 is false) and the normal case branch (baseReserve > 0 && quoteReserve > 0 is false). The shares variable remains 0, causing _mint(to, 0) to revert with ErrMintAmountNotEnough. This permanently blocks new deposits into the pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: If PMM pricing allows draining one reserve to zero (extreme swap), the pool becomes permanently unable to accept new liquidity. The dead shares (1001) ensure totalSupply > 0 even when all LP holders exit, creating an unrecoverable state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buyShares has only two branches for share calculation: totalSupply()==0 and (baseReserve > 0 && quoteReserve > 0); if totalSupply > 0 and one reserve is 0, shares stays 0 and _mint reverts

preconditions: One reserve can be depleted to zero via PMM swap (depends on K parameter)
Pool has dead shares (totalSupply = 1001 minimum)
All other LPs have exited or reserve drained via extreme swap

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No else branch handles totalSupply > 0 with one zero reserve; shares defaults to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Large swap to deplete one reserve, then exit LP position
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack - no direct profit but permanently disables pool deposits; trapped excess tokens benefit no one

economic_questions: Can PMM pricing actually produce outputs that drain one reserve to exactly 0?

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards notifyRewardAmount Epoch Boundary Reward Rate Spike
threats.hypothesis_description: In LockingMultiRewards.notifyRewardAmount(), if called with minRemainingTime=0 just seconds before the next epoch, the remainingRewardTime is very small. Leftover rewards from the current period are rolled into this tiny window: rewardRate = (newAmount + leftover) / remainingSeconds. This creates a massive temporary rewardRate that disproportionately benefits stakers who are already positioned, and punishes anyone who stakes even one second late.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reward rate calculation divides total rewards by remaining time. Near epoch boundary, remaining time approaches 0, creating reward rate spikes. An operator colluding with a staker could time this to maximize the staker's share.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reward.rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime where _remainingRewardTime = _nextEpoch - block.timestamp; when called 1 second before epoch, rate = total_amount / 1

preconditions: Operator calls notifyRewardAmount with minRemainingTime=0
Called very close to epoch boundary (seconds remaining)
Existing rewards in current period have leftover balance
Colluding staker has large position already staked

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If remainingTime=1 sec and leftover+new = 1000 tokens, rewardRate = 1000/sec; normal rate over 7 days would be 1000/604800 ≈ 0.00165/sec
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator times notification to minimize remainingTime, concentrating entire reward into brief window
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Staker captures nearly all rewards for the period if they hold majority of supply during the 1-second spike

threats.title: MagicLP setParameters Unsafe Narrowing Cast Silently Truncates Pool Parameters
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP.setParameters() stores _LP_FEE_RATE_ as uint64, _K_ as uint64, and _I_ as uint128 via explicit narrowing casts, despite the state variables being uint256. While current validation ranges fit within these types, the storage values after setParameters differ from after init() (which stores full uint256). If MAX_I, MAX_K, or MAX_LP_FEE_RATE constants are ever increased, values would be silently truncated without revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The narrowing casts are applied after validation but the types chosen (uint64/uint128) are smaller than the state variable types (uint256). This creates latent truncation risk and means setParameters effectively reduces the parameter precision compared to init().

proof_of_signal.pattern: _LP_FEE_RATE_ = uint64(newLpFeeRate); _K_ = uint64(newK); _I_ = uint128(newI); — narrowing cast on uint256 state variables

preconditions: Implementation owner calls setParameters

Future code changes increase MAX constants beyond uint64/uint128

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If MAX_I increases beyond uint128, setParameters silently truncates _I_, breaking pool pricing
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Silently truncated _I_ would cause massive pricing deviation, enabling arbitrage extraction

threats.title: MagicLP ratioSync Division by Zero When Reserve Is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP.ratioSync() adjusts targets proportionally to balance changes: _BASE_TARGET_ = uint112((uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * baseBalance) / uint256(_BASE_RESERVE_)). If _BASE_RESERVE_ is 0, this causes a division by zero revert. The implementation owner cannot recover the pool via ratioSync if a reserve is zero, compounding the buyShares DoS described in ECON-H004.
threats.investigation_rationale: ratioSync is an admin recovery function, but it has no zero-reserve guard. If a pool enters a state where one reserve is 0, the primary recovery mechanism (ratioSync) also fails, leaving only setParameters as a potential recovery path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _BASE_TARGET_ = uint112((uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * baseBalance) / uint256(_BASE_RESERVE_)); divides by _BASE_RESERVE_ with no zero check

preconditions: _BASE_RESERVE_ or _QUOTE_RESERVE_ is zero
Pool is in DoS state (ECON-H004 condition)
Implementation owner attempts recovery via ratioSync

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Division by _BASE_RESERVE_ when it could be 0; no guard: if (_BASE_RESERVE_ == 0) return;
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After reserve depletion, admin cannot use ratioSync for recovery
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Strengthens DoS attack by removing admin recovery path

threats.title: Router.createPoolETH uses safeTransferFrom for WETH held by Router, causing pool creation to revert and potentially strand user ETH expectations
threats.hypothesis_description: In createPoolETH(), the router wraps ETH into WETH, then attempts to move WETH to the clone using `address(weth).safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)`. Since Router has not approved itself, the WETH `transferFrom` will generally fail. This makes createPoolETH unusable and can break bootstrapping/liquidity flows that rely on it (especially during operational launches).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect affecting a core periphery function. If relied upon in production, it creates an operational DoS for ETH-based pool creation. While the call reverts (so ETH is not lost), the workflow failure is exploitable as an availability attack (spam/force repeated failures) and can stall launches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createPoolETH(): weth.deposit{value: msg.value}(); then `address(weth).safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)` (should be safeTransfer)

Users/ops attempt to create pools using ETH path (createPoolETH)

threats.title: BlastOnboarding.proxy bootstrapper unset or set to EOA can brick delegated functionality and confuse user flows
threats.hypothesis_description: BlastOnboarding is an OZ Proxy that delegates to `bootstrapper`. If bootstrapper is unset (address(0)) or set incorrectly (non-contract/EOA), any fallback-delegated calls will fail. While deposit/withdraw are implemented directly on the proxy contract, the bootstrapping/claim lifecycle relies on upgrading bootstrapper to BlastOnboardingBoot. Mis-setting bootstrapper can stall claiming or bootstrapping at critical times.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a high-impact operational misconfiguration risk around upgrades. It’s not an off-chain key compromise scenario; rather, it’s a concrete configuration dependency that must be validated against deployment scripts and upgrade runbooks.
deployment_assumptions: Owner intends to use delegatecall-based implementation (BlastOnboardingBoot) for bootstrap/claim
Bootstrapper is set post-deploy via setBootstrapper()

bootstrapper is zero or not a valid contract implementing required selectors

threats.title: BlastOnboardingBoot setReady Can Be Toggled After Users Claim
threats.hypothesis_description: setReady() allows the owner to toggle ready between true and false at any time (state must be Closed). After some users claim with ready=true, the owner can set ready=false, change the staking contract via setStaking() (which only blocks when ready=true), then set ready=true again. threats.title: BlastOnboarding proxy can be bricked or have funds stolen via owner setting bootstrapper to malicious implementation (delegatecall abuse)

threats.title: Fee Rate Manipulation via tx.origin Phishing
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP uses `tx.origin` instead of `msg.sender` to determine the fee rate for swaps, allowing an attacker to bypass contract-specific fees or hijack a VIP user's discounted fee rate via phishing.

By using `tx.origin`, the fee rate is based on the EOA that signed the transaction, not the immediate calling contract. If the FeeRateModel provides discounts to specific EOAs, an attacker can deploy a malicious contract and trick a VIP user into calling it. Additionally, this breaks compatibility with Account Abstraction (ERC-4337) where `tx.origin` is the bundler.

threats.title: Incorrect State Validation in FeeCollectable Allows Setting Unlimited Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setFeeParameters` function in `FeeCollectable` validates the old state variable `feeBips` instead of the new input parameter `_feeBips` (`if (feeBips > BIPS)`). On the first call, `feeBips` is 0, so the check passes regardless of the value of `_feeBips`. A malicious or compromised fee operator can set `_feeBips` to a value greater than 10,000 (100%). Subsequent calculations in `calculateFees` will underflow when subtracting the fee from the input amount, causing a permanent Denial of Service for any contract inheriting this logic.
threats.investigation_rationale: While requiring operator privileges, this is a critical code defect that completely breaks the intended bounds checking, allowing an operator to permanently brick the contract.

threats.title: Owner Can Toggle Ready State and Fragment Staking Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setReady` function in `BlastOnboardingBoot` allows the owner to toggle the `ready` state back to `false` after users have already claimed shares. The owner can then use `setStaking` to change the staking contract and set `ready` back to `true`. Early claimers will have their shares locked in the old staking contract, while new claimers will use the new one. This fragments liquidity and may orphan early users from future reward distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: State transitions that should be one-way are reversible, allowing admins to disrupt the protocol's intended operation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check preventing ready from being set to false after claims

preconditions: Owner acts maliciously or makes an operational error

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reversible state transition for a one-time bootstrapping event.

operational_questions: Is there any legitimate reason to change the staking contract after claims have started?
How would early claimers migrate to the new staking contract?
validation_requirements: Call setReady(true), claim shares, call setReady(false), setStaking(newStaking), setReady(true)
Verify liquidity is fragmented

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users claim and stake in Contract A
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner sets ready=false, changes staking to Contract B, sets ready=true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: New users claim and stake in Contract B. Contract A is abandoned.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fragmentation of staking liquidity and orphaned users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual migration of users

threats.title: ProxyOracle can be set to a non-contract oracleImplementation, causing system-wide oracle DoS where used
threats.hypothesis_description: ProxyOracle.changeOracleImplementation does not validate that `newOracle` has code or implements the expected interface. If set to an EOA or incompatible contract, all calls (get/peek/peekSpot/decimals) will revert, potentially bricking any Cauldron or subsystem relying on that oracle for exchange rates and solvency checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a sharp-edge admin operation with high blast radius where ProxyOracle is used. It is a code-level missing validation but exploitation requires privileged access (owner).
deployment_assumptions: ProxyOracle is used as oracle in one or more Cauldrons or critical pricing paths
Owner performs oracle upgrades/rotations
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence in provided scripts that ProxyOracle is used or configured; hypothesis applies if integrated post-deploy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: changeOracleImplementation(): sets oracleImplementation without code/interface validation; downstream functions blindly call oracleImplementation.*

preconditions: ProxyOracle owner sets oracleImplementation incorrectly (EOA or incompatible oracle)
A dependent contract calls ProxyOracle.get/peek/peekSpot during critical flow (borrow/liquidate/solvency check)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Oracle indirection lacks sanity checks; misconfiguration leads to revert cascades.

operational_questions: Is ProxyOracle used anywhere in deployed Cauldrons (oracle address points to ProxyOracle)?
Are there runbooks/tests ensuring newOracle is a contract and returns sane values?
validation_requirements: Static scan of deployments for ProxyOracle usage
Simulate setting oracleImplementation to EOA and observe failure modes in Cauldron.updateExchangeRate/accrue flows

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If privileged: set oracleImplementation to invalid address

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium-High: core lending actions can halt if oracle calls revert
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires corrective owner tx; may be blocked if other dependencies fail)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: OracleImplementationChange events; sudden oracle call reverts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spike in failed borrows/liquidations due to oracle failure
